sentences.chinese: 卷六十一 張騫李廣利傳第三十一
sentences.literal: Volume 61: Biographies the Thirty-first, Zhang Qian and Li Guangli.
sentences.idiomatic: Volume 61: Biography 31—Zhang Qian and Li Guangli.

sentences.chinese: 張騫，漢中人也，建元中為郎。
sentences.literal: Zhang Qian was a man of Hanzhong; during the Jianyuan era he served as a gentleman-at-court.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Qian came from Hanzhong and held a courtier's post in the Jianyuan years.

sentences.chinese: 時，匈奴降者言匈奴破月氏王，以其頭為飲器，月氏遁而怨匈奴，無與共擊之。
sentences.literal: At that time, defectors from the Xiongnu said the Xiongnu had defeated the king of the Yuezhi and made his skull into a drinking vessel; the Yuezhi had fled and resented the Xiongnu, but had no one to join them in striking the Xiongnu.
sentences.idiomatic: Defectors from the Xiongnu reported that the nomads had crushed the Yuezhi king and turned his skull into a cup; the Yuezhi had withdrawn in bitterness, with no ally willing to help them strike back.

sentences.chinese: 漢方欲事滅胡，聞此言，欲通使，道必更匈奴中，乃募能使者。
sentences.literal: Han was at that time intent on destroying the Hu; hearing these words, it wished to send envoys, but the route had necessarily to pass through the Xiongnu, so it recruited one who could go.
sentences.idiomatic: The court was bent on breaking the northern tribes; on hearing this, it wanted to open contact with the Yuezhi, yet any road there ran through Xiongnu territory, so it called for a man willing to make the journey.

sentences.chinese: 騫以郎應募，使月氏，與堂邑氏奴甘父俱出隴西。
sentences.literal: Qian, as a gentleman-at-court, answered the recruitment and was sent to the Yuezhi, together with the Tangyi clan's slave Ganfu, both departing from Longxi.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Qian, a courtier, volunteered for the mission to the Yuezhi and set out from Longxi with his guide Ganfu, a Tangyi household slave.

sentences.chinese: 徑匈奴，匈奴得之，傳詣單于。
sentences.literal: They went straight through the Xiongnu; the Xiongnu seized them and conveyed them to the chanyu.
sentences.idiomatic: They cut across Xiongnu lands, were taken prisoner, and were escorted to the chanyu.

sentences.chinese: 單于曰：「月氏在吾北，漢何以得往使？
sentences.literal: The chanyu said, "The Yuezhi are to my north; how can Han go and send envoys there?
sentences.idiomatic: The chanyu asked, "The Yuezhi lie north of me—how does Han imagine it can send envoys past us?

sentences.chinese: 吾欲使越，漢肯聽我乎？」
sentences.literal: If I wished to send envoys to Yue, would Han listen to me?"
sentences.idiomatic: If I wanted an embassy to cross Han territory into Yue, would your court allow it?"

sentences.chinese: 留騫十餘歲，予妻，有子，然騫持漢節不失。
sentences.literal: He detained Qian for more than ten years, gave him a wife, and he had a son, yet Qian held fast to the Han credentials and did not lose them.
sentences.idiomatic: Qian was held over ten years, given a wife and a child, but he never let go of his Han staff of office.

sentences.chinese: 居匈奴西，騫因與其屬亡鄉月氏，西走數十日，至大宛。
sentences.literal: Dwelling west of the Xiongnu, Qian thereupon with his followers fled, aiming for the Yuezhi; going west for several tens of days, they reached Dayuan.
sentences.idiomatic: Stationed west of the nomads, he slipped away with his party toward the Yuezhi; after many days' march westward they reached Dayuan.

sentences.chinese: 大宛聞漢之饒財，欲通不得，見騫，喜，問欲何之。
sentences.literal: Dayuan had heard that Han was rich in goods and wished to open communication but could not; seeing Qian, it rejoiced and asked where he wished to go.
sentences.idiomatic: Dayuan had long heard of Han's wealth and wanted trade but could not reach it; delighted to see Qian, the king asked his destination.

sentences.chinese: 騫曰：「為漢使月氏而為匈奴所閉道，今亡，唯王使人道送我。
sentences.literal: Qian said, "I was sent by Han to the Yuezhi but the Xiongnu blocked the road; now I have fled; I only ask that the king send men to guide and escort me.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Qian said, "Han sent me to the Yuezhi; the Xiongnu cut the route. I have escaped—only ask that you provide guides to see me through."

sentences.chinese: 誠得至，反漢，漢之賂遺王財物不可勝言。」
sentences.literal: If I truly reach there and return to Han, Han's gifts and presents to the king in goods cannot be told in full."
sentences.idiomatic: If I reach them and return, the gifts Han will send your majesty will be beyond reckoning."

sentences.chinese: 大宛以為然，遣騫，為發道譯，抵康居。
sentences.literal: Dayuan deemed it right, sent Qian off, and for him issued guides and interpreters until they reached Kangju.
sentences.idiomatic: The king agreed, furnished interpreters and an escort, and sent him on to Kangju.

sentences.chinese: 康居傳致大月氏。
sentences.literal: Kangju passed him on until he reached the Greater Yuezhi.
sentences.idiomatic: From Kangju he was relayed onward to the Greater Yuezhi.

sentences.chinese: 大月氏王已為胡所殺，立其夫人為王。
sentences.literal: The king of the Greater Yuezhi had already been killed by the Hu, and his consort was established as ruler.
sentences.idiomatic: The Yuezhi king had fallen to the nomads; his queen now ruled in his place.

sentences.chinese: 既臣大夏而君之，地肥饒，少寇，志安樂。
sentences.literal: Having already made Daxia subject and ruled it, the land was fertile and rich, bandits were few, and their intent was peace and pleasure.
sentences.idiomatic: They had subdued Daxia and held its rich soil with little raiding; they were settled and had no wish for war.

sentences.chinese: 又自以遠遠漢，殊無報胡之心。
sentences.literal: Moreover they themselves considered themselves far, far from Han and altogether had no mind to repay the Hu.
sentences.idiomatic: They also reckoned themselves impossibly distant from Han and felt no urge to avenge themselves on the Xiongnu.

sentences.chinese: 騫從月氏至大夏，竟不能得月氏要領。
sentences.literal: Qian followed the Yuezhi to Daxia and in the end could not obtain the Yuezhi's main point.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Qian went on with them into Daxia but never secured a firm commitment from the Yuezhi.

sentences.chinese: 留歲餘，還，並南山，欲從羌中歸，復為匈奴所得。
sentences.literal: He stayed over a year, then returned, keeping south of the mountains, intending to return through the Qiang region, and again was taken by the Xiongnu.
sentences.idiomatic: After more than a year he turned homeward along the southern ranges, hoping to slip through Qiang country, only to be seized again by the Xiongnu.

sentences.chinese: 留歲餘，單于死，國內亂，騫與胡妻及堂邑父俱亡歸漢。
sentences.literal: He was detained over a year; the chanyu died and the state was in turmoil within; Qian together with his Hu wife and Tangyi Fu all fled and returned to Han.
sentences.idiomatic: Another year passed; the chanyu died and the realm fell into strife, and Zhang Qian fled to Han with his Xiongnu wife and Ganfu.

sentences.chinese: 拜騫太中大夫，堂邑父為奉使君。
sentences.literal: Qian was appointed grand counsellor of the palace; Tangyi Fu was made Fengshi lord.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor appointed Zhang Qian grand counsellor of the palace and ennobled Ganfu as Lord Fengshi for his part in the embassy.

sentences.chinese: 騫為人強力，寬大信人，蠻夷愛之。
sentences.literal: Qian as a person was forceful, broad-minded, and trusted others; the barbarians loved him.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Qian was resolute, generous, and trustworthy; frontier peoples warmed to him.

sentences.chinese: 堂邑父胡人，善射，窮急射禽獸給食。
sentences.literal: Tangyi Fu was a Hu man, skilled at archery; in extremity he shot birds and beasts to supply food.
sentences.idiomatic: Ganfu, a northerner, was a fine bowman who, when supplies ran out, brought down game to keep them fed.

sentences.chinese: 初，騫行時百餘人，去十三歲，唯二人得還。
sentences.literal: At first, when Qian set out there were over a hundred persons; he was gone thirteen years; only two persons were able to return.
sentences.idiomatic: He had left with more than a hundred men; thirteen years later only two came back.

sentences.chinese: 騫身所至者，大宛、大月氏、大夏、康居，而傳聞其旁大國五六，具為天子言其地形所有，語皆在《西域傳》。
sentences.literal: As for places Qian himself reached, there were Dayuan, the Greater Yuezhi, Daxia, and Kangju, and by hearsay five or six great states beside them; he fully reported to the Son of Heaven their terrain and what they possessed; the words are all in the "Treatise on the Western Regions."
sentences.idiomatic: In person he had reached Dayuan, the Greater Yuezhi, Daxia, and Kangju, and by report knew five or six other great kingdoms nearby; he laid all of this—lands and resources—before the emperor, as recorded in the Western Regions treatise.

sentences.chinese: 騫曰：「臣在大夏時，見邛竹杖、蜀布，問：『安得此？』
sentences.literal: Qian said, "When your servant was in Daxia, I saw Qiong bamboo staffs and Shu cloth and asked, 'How did these come here?'"
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Qian said, "In Daxia I saw bamboo canes from Qiong and cloth from Shu and asked how such things had got there."

sentences.chinese: 大夏國人曰：『吾賈人往市之身毒國。
sentences.literal: The people of Daxia said, 'Our merchants went to buy them in the Shendu state.'
sentences.idiomatic: They answered, 'Our traders brought them back from Shendu (India).'

sentences.chinese: 身毒國在大夏東南可數千里。
sentences.literal: The Shendu state is in Daxia's southeast, perhaps several thousand li.
sentences.idiomatic: Shendu lies some thousands of li southeast of Daxia.

sentences.chinese: 其俗土著，與大夏同，而卑濕暑熱。
sentences.literal: Its customs are sedentary, the same as Daxia's, but low-lying, damp, and hot in summer.
sentences.idiomatic: The people are settled like those of Daxia, but the land is low, humid, and sweltering.

sentences.chinese: 其民乘象以戰。
sentences.literal: Its people mount elephants to fight.
sentences.idiomatic: They fight from the backs of elephants.

sentences.chinese: 其國臨大水焉。』
sentences.literal: Its state borders a great water.'"
sentences.idiomatic: Their country fronts on a great river.'"

sentences.chinese: 以騫度之，大夏去漢萬二千里，居西南。
sentences.literal: By Qian's reckoning, Daxia is twelve thousand li from Han and lies in the southwest.
sentences.idiomatic: By Zhang Qian's estimate Daxia stood twelve thousand li from the capital, to the southwest.

sentences.chinese: 今身毒又居大夏東南數千里，有蜀物，此其去蜀不遠矣。
sentences.literal: Now Shendu again lies several thousand li southeast of Daxia and has Shu goods; this means it is not far from Shu.
sentences.idiomatic: Shendu lies thousands of li beyond Daxia to the southeast yet already has Shu goods—so it cannot be far from Shu itself.

sentences.chinese: 今使大夏，從羌中，險，羌人惡之；
sentences.literal: Now to send envoys to Daxia through the Qiang region is perilous, and the Qiang detest it;
sentences.idiomatic: The route to Daxia through Qiang territory is dangerous, and the Qiang resent intruders;

sentences.chinese: 少北，則為匈奴所得；
sentences.literal: a little to the north, then one is taken by the Xiongnu;
sentences.idiomatic: veer slightly north and the Xiongnu take you;

sentences.chinese: 從蜀，宜徑，又無寇。」
sentences.literal: from Shu it should be a direct route and moreover without raiders."
sentences.idiomatic: from Shu the way should be straighter and free of marauders."

sentences.chinese: 天子既聞大宛及大夏、安息之屬皆大國，多奇物，土著，頗與中國同俗，而兵弱，貴漢財物；
sentences.literal: The Son of Heaven having heard that Dayuan and Daxia, Anxi, and the like were all great states, with many strange goods, sedentary populations, customs rather like China's, yet weak in arms and valuing Han goods;
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor had learned that Dayuan, Daxia, Parthia, and their neighbors were large, settled kingdoms rich in curiosities, with customs not unlike China's, weak armies, and a hunger for Han wealth;

sentences.chinese: 其北則大月氏、康居之屬，兵強，可以賂遺設利朝也。
sentences.literal: to their north were the Greater Yuezhi, Kangju, and the like, strong in arms, who could be drawn by gifts and profit to court audience.
sentences.idiomatic: north of them lay the Greater Yuezhi and Kangju—hard fighters who might nonetheless be courted with bribes and profit.

sentences.chinese: 誠得而以義屬之，則廣地萬里，重九譯，致殊俗，威德遍於四海。
sentences.literal: If truly obtained and by righteousness made subject, then lands would extend ten thousand li, repeated ninefold interpretation would reach strange customs, and awe-inspiring virtue would spread over the four seas.
sentences.idiomatic: If they could be bound in good faith to the throne, Han might stretch ten thousand li, speak through chains of interpreters to distant peoples, and spread its majesty to the ends of the earth.

sentences.chinese: 天子欣欣以騫言為然。
sentences.literal: The Son of Heaven gladly deemed Qian's words correct.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor was delighted and accepted Zhang Qian's counsel.

sentences.chinese: 乃令因蜀犍為發間使，四道並出：出駹，出莋，出徙、邛，出僰，皆各行一二千里。
sentences.literal: Thereupon he ordered that from Shu and Jianwei secret envoys be dispatched, four routes going out together: out by Mang, out by Zuo, out by Xi and Qiong, out by Bo, each going one or two thousand li.
sentences.idiomatic: He ordered secret missions from Shu and Jianwei along four routes—through Mang, Zuo, Xi and Qiong, and Bo—each probing one or two thousand li.

sentences.chinese: 其北方閉氐、莋，南方閉巂、昆明。
sentences.literal: On the northern side they were blocked by the Di and Zuo; on the southern side blocked by Xi and Kunming.
sentences.idiomatic: The Di and Zuo blocked the northern parties; the Xi and Kunming barred the southern ones.

sentences.chinese: 昆明之屬無君長，善寇盜，輒殺略漢使，終莫得通。
sentences.literal: The Kunming sort had no rulers or chiefs, were good at raiding and plundering, repeatedly killed and robbed Han envoys, and in the end passage could not be obtained.
sentences.idiomatic: The Kunming peoples had no chiefs, lived by raiding, killed and plundered Han envoys, and no road was ever opened through them.

sentences.chinese: 然聞其西可千餘里，有乘象國，名滇越，而蜀賈間出物者或至焉，於是漢以求大復道始通滇國。
sentences.literal: Yet it was heard that west of them perhaps over a thousand li there was an elephant-riding state named Dianyue, and Shu merchants who occasionally went out with goods sometimes reached there; thereupon Han, in seeking the road to Daxia, first opened communication with the Dian state.
sentences.idiomatic: Word had it that a thousand li west lay an elephant-riding realm called Dianyue, where Shu merchants sometimes appeared; in pursuit of a road to Daxia, Han thus opened relations with Dian for the first time.

sentences.chinese: 初，漢欲通西南夷，費多，罷之。
sentences.literal: At first, Han wished to open communication with the southwestern Yi, but the expense was great and it abandoned this.
sentences.idiomatic: Earlier attempts to reach the southwestern tribes had been dropped as too costly.

sentences.chinese: 及騫言可以通大夏，及復事西南夷。
sentences.literal: When Qian said one could reach Daxia, then again they took up affairs with the southwestern Yi.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Qian's report that Daxia could be reached from the southwest revived the whole frontier policy.

sentences.chinese: 騫以校尉從大將軍擊匈奴，知水草處，軍得以不乏，乃封騫為博望侯。
sentences.literal: Qian as colonel followed the grand general in striking the Xiongnu; he knew the places of water and grass, so the army was able not to lack supplies, and thereupon Qian was enfeoffed as marquis of Bowang.
sentences.idiomatic: As a colonel under the grand general he guided the army to water and pasture so the troops did not want; for this he was enfeoffed as Marquis of Bowang.

sentences.chinese: 是歲，元朔六年也。
sentences.literal: That year was the sixth year of Yuanshuo.
sentences.idiomatic: This was the sixth year of Yuanshuo (123 BCE).

sentences.chinese: 後二年，騫為衛尉，與李廣俱出右北平擊匈奴。
sentences.literal: After two years, Qian was commandant of the guards and together with Li Guang both went out of Youbeiping to strike the Xiongnu.
sentences.idiomatic: Two years later, as commandant of the guards, he joined Li Guang in a sortie from Youbeiping against the Xiongnu.

sentences.chinese: 匈奴圍李將軍，軍失亡多，而騫後期當斬，贖為庶人。
sentences.literal: The Xiongnu surrounded General Li; the army lost and fled many, while Qian was late to the rendezvous and deserved execution; he ransomed himself and became a commoner.
sentences.idiomatic: The Xiongnu encircled Li Guang's command with heavy losses; Zhang Qian arrived late, faced capital sentence, ransomed his rank away, and was reduced to commoner status.

sentences.chinese: 是歲，驃騎將軍破匈奴西邊，殺數萬人，至祁連山。
sentences.literal: That year the flying-charge general broke the Xiongnu western border, killed several tens of thousands, and reached the Qilian Mountains.
sentences.idiomatic: That same year the flying-charge general shattered the western Xiongnu, killing tens of thousands and pushing to the Qilian range.

sentences.chinese: 其秋，渾邪王率眾降漢，而金城、河西並南山至鹽澤，空無匈奴。
sentences.literal: That autumn the king of Hunye led his masses to surrender to Han, and from Jincheng and Hexi along the southern mountains to the Salt Marsh it was empty of Xiongnu.
sentences.idiomatic: That autumn Hunye king came over with his people, and from Jincheng and the Hexi corridor south to the salt lakes the steppe lay empty of nomads.

sentences.chinese: 匈奴時有候者到，而希矣。
sentences.literal: The Xiongnu at times had scouts arrive, but they were rare.
sentences.idiomatic: Occasional Xiongnu scouts still appeared, but they were few.

sentences.chinese: 後二年，漢擊走單于於幕北。
sentences.literal: After two years, Han struck and drove the chanyu north of the desert curtain.
sentences.idiomatic: Two years later Han drove the chanyu beyond the northern desert.

sentences.chinese: 天子數問騫大夏之屬。
sentences.literal: The Son of Heaven repeatedly asked Qian about Daxia and the like.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor often pressed Zhang Qian for news of Daxia and the western states.

sentences.chinese: 騫既失侯，因曰：「臣居匈奴中，聞烏孫王號昆莫。
sentences.literal: Qian having lost his marquisate, thereupon said, "When your servant dwelt among the Xiongnu, I heard the Wusun king is titled Kunmo.
sentences.idiomatic: Stripped of his fief, Zhang Qian said, "Among the Xiongnu I heard that the Wusun ruler bears the title Kunmo."

sentences.chinese: 昆莫父難兜靡本與大月氏俱在祁連、敦煌間，小國也。
sentences.literal: Kunmo's father Nandoumi originally together with the Greater Yuezhi were between Qilian and Dunhuang, a small state.
sentences.idiomatic: Kunmo's father Nandoumi had ruled a small people between the Qilian range and Dunhuang alongside the Greater Yuezhi.

sentences.chinese: 大月氏攻殺難兜靡，奪其地，人民亡走匈奴。
sentences.literal: The Greater Yuezhi attacked and killed Nandoumi, seized his land, and the people fled in disorder to the Xiongnu.
sentences.idiomatic: The Greater Yuezhi slew Nandoumi, seized his territory, and his followers fled to the Xiongnu.

sentences.chinese: 子昆莫新生，傅父布就翕侯抱亡置草中，為求食，還，見狼乳之，又烏銜肉翔其旁，以為神，遂持歸匈奴，單于愛養之。
sentences.literal: The son Kunmo was newly born; the foster father Bu-jiu Xihou took him in flight, placed him in the grass, went to seek food, returned, saw a wolf suckling him, and moreover a crow holding meat circling beside him; deeming him divine, he then carried him back to the Xiongnu, and the chanyu cherished and raised him.
sentences.idiomatic: The infant Kunmo was carried off by his nurse-father Bu-jiu the Xihou, hidden in the grass while food was sought; returning, the man found a wolf nursing the child and crows dropping meat nearby. Convinced the boy was spirit-touched, he brought him to the chanyu, who reared him with favor.

sentences.chinese: 及壯，以其父民眾與昆莫，使將兵，數有功。
sentences.literal: When he was grown, the chanyu gave him his father's people and masses, ordered him to lead troops, and several times he had merit.
sentences.idiomatic: When the boy came of age, the chanyu restored his father's tribesmen to him, gave him a command, and he won repeated distinction.

sentences.chinese: 時，月氏已為匈奴所破，西擊塞王。
sentences.literal: At that time the Yuezhi had already been broken by the Xiongnu and went west to strike the Sai king.
sentences.idiomatic: By then the Xiongnu had shattered the Yuezhi, who marched west against the Sai king.

sentences.chinese: 塞王南走遠徙，月氏居其地。
sentences.literal: The Sai king fled south and removed far; the Yuezhi dwelt in his land.
sentences.idiomatic: The Sai king withdrew far to the south, and the Yuezhi took his ground.

sentences.chinese: 昆莫既健，自請單于報父怨，遂西攻破大月氏。
sentences.literal: Kunmo having become strong, himself requested of the chanyu to repay his father's grudge, and thereupon went west and broke the Greater Yuezhi.
sentences.idiomatic: Once Kunmo grew strong he begged leave to avenge his father; marching west, he crushed the Greater Yuezhi.

sentences.chinese: 大月氏復西走，徒大夏地。
sentences.literal: The Greater Yuezhi again fled west and moved to Daxia's territory.
sentences.idiomatic: The Yuezhi fled farther west and settled on Daxia's soil.

sentences.chinese: 昆莫略其眾，因留居，兵稍強，會單于死，不肯復朝事匈奴。
sentences.literal: Kunmo plundered their masses, thereupon stayed and dwelt, arms gradually grew strong, it happened the chanyu died, and he was unwilling again to attend court and serve the Xiongnu.
sentences.idiomatic: Kunmo absorbed their warriors, stayed put, and grew in strength; when the chanyu died he refused ever again to acknowledge Xiongnu overlordship.

sentences.chinese: 匈奴遣兵擊之，不勝，益以為神而遠之。
sentences.literal: The Xiongnu sent troops to strike him, did not prevail, and increasingly deemed him divine and kept distant from him.
sentences.idiomatic: Xiongnu attacks failed; they came to regard him as uncanny and gave him a wide berth.

sentences.chinese: 今單于新困於漢，而昆莫地空。
sentences.literal: Now the chanyu has newly been distressed by Han, while Kunmo's land is empty.
sentences.idiomatic: The chanyu is newly worsted by Han, and Kunmo's old pasture lands lie open.

sentences.chinese: 蠻夷戀故地，又貪漢物，誠以此時厚賂烏孫，招以東居故地，漢遣公主為夫人，結昆弟，其勢宜聽，則是斷匈奴右臂也。
sentences.literal: The barbarian Yi cling to old lands and moreover covet Han goods; if truly at this time one richly bribes Wusun, summons them to dwell east in the old lands, Han sends a princess as consort, binds brotherhood, by the situation they ought to listen—then this is cutting off the Xiongnu's right arm.
sentences.idiomatic: Frontier peoples cling to ancestral ground yet hunger for Han goods; rich gifts now could draw the Wusun back east to their old pastures, a Han princess sealing a brotherly pact—they would surely listen, and you would sever the Xiongnu's right arm.

sentences.chinese: 既連烏孫，自其西大夏之屬皆可招來而為外臣。」
sentences.literal: Having already linked Wusun, from west of them Daxia and the like can all be summoned and come to be outer subjects."
sentences.idiomatic: Once Wusun is bound to us, Daxia and the states beyond can be summoned as outer vassals."

sentences.chinese: 天子以為然，拜騫為中郎將，將三百人，馬各二匹，牛、羊以萬數，繼金幣帛直數千巨萬，多持節副使，道可便遣之旁國。
sentences.literal: The Son of Heaven deemed it right, appointed Qian general of the gentlemen of the household, leading three hundred men, each with two horses, cattle and sheep in tens of thousands, carrying gold, coins, and silks worth several thousand times ten thousand, many holding credentials as deputy envoys, on the road being able as convenient to dispatch them to neighboring states.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor agreed, named Zhang Qian general of the household with three hundred followers, two mounts each, vast herds of stock, and treasure worth tens of millions in cash and silk, with many credentialled deputies who could be sent on to neighboring courts as the route allowed.

sentences.chinese: 騫既至烏孫，致賜諭指，未能得其決。
sentences.literal: Qian having reached Wusun, conveyed gifts and explained intent, was not able to obtain their decision.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Qian reached Wusun with gifts and the emperor's message but could not secure a firm answer.

sentences.chinese: 語在《西域傳》。
sentences.literal: The words are in the "Treatise on the Western Regions."
sentences.idiomatic: The details are given in the Western Regions treatise.

sentences.chinese: 騫即分遣副使使大宛、康居、月氏、大夏。
sentences.literal: Qian thereupon separately dispatched deputy envoys to go to Dayuan, Kangju, the Yuezhi, and Daxia.
sentences.idiomatic: He at once sent deputies on to Dayuan, Kangju, the Yuezhi, and Daxia.

sentences.chinese: 烏孫發道譯送騫，與烏孫使數十人，馬數十匹。
sentences.literal: Wusun issued guides and interpreters to escort Qian, together with several tens of Wusun envoys and several tens of horses.
sentences.idiomatic: The Wusun furnished guides to escort him home with dozens of their envoys and mounts.

sentences.chinese: 報謝，因令窺漢，知其廣大。
sentences.literal: They returned thanks; thereupon Han had them observe Han and know its vastness.
sentences.idiomatic: They came to offer thanks, and the court had them tour the realm until they understood how vast Han was.

sentences.chinese: 騫還，拜為大行。
sentences.literal: Qian returned and was appointed grand coachman.
sentences.idiomatic: On his return Zhang Qian was appointed grand coachman.

sentences.chinese: 歲餘，騫卒。
sentences.literal: After more than a year, Qian died.
sentences.idiomatic: A little over a year later he died.

sentences.chinese: 後歲餘，其所遣副使通大夏之屬者皆頗與其人俱來，於是西北國始通於漢矣。
sentences.literal: After more than a year, those deputy envoys he had dispatched who had opened communication with Daxia and the like all largely came together with their people; thereupon the northwestern states first opened communication with Han.
sentences.idiomatic: Within another year the deputies he had sent toward Daxia began returning with foreign retinues, and the northwestern kingdoms were at last in steady contact with Han.

sentences.chinese: 然騫鑿空，諸後使往者皆稱博望侯，以為質於外國，外國由是信之。
sentences.literal: Yet Qian bored the void; all later envoys who went all called themselves marquis of Bowang, using this as pledge in foreign states, and foreign states thereby trusted them.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Qian had opened the blank on the map; later envoys all called themselves the Marquis of Bowang to win trust abroad, and the western courts believed them for it.

sentences.chinese: 其後，烏孫竟與漢結婚。
sentences.literal: After that, Wusun in the end allied in marriage with Han.
sentences.idiomatic: In time the Wusun did take a Han princess in marriage.

sentences.chinese: 初，天子發書《易》，曰「神馬當從西北來」。
sentences.literal: At first, the Son of Heaven opened the document Changes, and it said, "The divine horse shall come from the northwest."
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor had once consulted the Changes and read the omen: "A spirit horse will come from the northwest."

sentences.chinese: 得烏孫馬好，名曰：「天馬」。
sentences.literal: Obtaining Wusun horses that were fine, they named them "heavenly horses."
sentences.idiomatic: The fine Wusun mounts were dubbed "heavenly horses."

sentences.chinese: 及得宛汗血馬，益壯，更名烏孫馬曰「西極馬」，宛馬曰「天馬」云。
sentences.literal: When they obtained Ferghana blood-sweating horses, still more robust, they changed the name of Wusun horses to "western-pole horses" and Ferghana horses to "heavenly horses," it is said.
sentences.idiomatic: When the blood-sweating Ferghana horses arrived—still hardier—the Wusun stock was renamed "horses of the western pole" and the Ferghana animals alone kept the title "heavenly horses."

sentences.chinese: 而漢始築令居以西，初置酒泉郡，以通西北國。
sentences.literal: And Han for the first time built west of Lingju, first established Jiuquan commandery, in order to open communication with the northwestern states.
sentences.idiomatic: Han began building the wall west of Lingju and founded Jiuquan commandery to hold the road open to the northwest.

sentences.chinese: 因《益》發使抵安息、奄蔡、□、條支、身毒國。
sentences.literal: On the basis of the Changes (hexagram Yi), envoys were dispatched reaching Anxi, Yancai, a state whose name is missing in the text (□), Tiaozhi, and Shendu.
sentences.idiomatic: Taking their cue from the Changes, the court sent missions to Parthia, Yancai, a kingdom the manuscript leaves blank, Characene on the Persian Gulf, and Shendu (India).

sentences.chinese: 而天子好宛馬，使者相望於道，一輩大者數百，少者百餘人，所繼操，大放博望侯時。
sentences.literal: And the Son of Heaven loved Ferghana horses; envoys thronged the roads; a large party numbered several hundred, a small one still over a hundred; the goods and equipage they carried in train far exceeded what had been sent in the Marquis of Bowang's day.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor's passion for Ferghana horses filled the highways with embassies—some missions ran to hundreds of men, none to fewer than a hundred—with baggage trains dwarfing Zhang Qian's first expedition.

sentences.chinese: 其後益習而衰少焉。
sentences.literal: After that they increasingly became practiced and declined and diminished.
sentences.idiomatic: Later, as the routes grew familiar, the traffic thinned.

sentences.chinese: 漢率一歲中使者多者十餘，少者五六輩，遠者八九歲，近者數歲而反。
sentences.literal: Han as a rule in one year had envoys in the many case over ten, in the few case five or six parties; those far took eight or nine years, those near several years and returned.
sentences.idiomatic: In a typical year Han sent ten or more missions, or as few as five or six; the long journeys took eight or nine years, shorter ones several years, before men returned.

sentences.chinese: 是時，漢既滅越，蜀所通西南夷皆震，請吏。
sentences.literal: At this time, Han having already destroyed Yue, the southwestern Yi communicated with by Shu all trembled and requested officials.
sentences.idiomatic: Han had just crushed Nanyue; the southwestern tribes reached from Shu were awed and begged to be placed under Han magistrates.

sentences.chinese: 置牂柯、越巂、益州、沈黎、文山郡，欲地接以前通大夏。
sentences.literal: They established Zangke, Yuexi, Yizhou, Shenli, and Wenshan commanderies, desiring the lands to connect so as previously to open communication with Daxia.
sentences.idiomatic: The court set up Zangke, Yuexi, Yizhou, Shenli, and Wenshan commanderies, pushing frontiers forward to link with the road toward Daxia.

sentences.chinese: 乃遣使歲十餘輩，出此初郡，皆復閉昆明，為所殺，奪幣物。
sentences.literal: Thereupon they dispatched envoys year after year over ten parties, going out from these newly established commanderies, all again being blocked by Kunming, by them being killed, and gifts and goods seized.
sentences.idiomatic: Year after year a dozen or more missions left these new commands, only to be stopped by the Kunming peoples, who killed the envoys and seized their goods.

sentences.chinese: 於是漢發兵擊昆明，斬首數萬。
sentences.literal: Thereupon Han raised troops to strike Kunming and beheaded several tens of thousands.
sentences.idiomatic: Han then sent an army against Kunming and claimed tens of thousands of heads.

sentences.chinese: 後復遣使，竟不得通。
sentences.literal: After again dispatching envoys, in the end passage could not be obtained.
sentences.idiomatic: Further embassies still failed to open the road.

sentences.chinese: 語在《西南夷傳》。
sentences.literal: The words are in the "Treatise on the Southwestern Yi."
sentences.idiomatic: The story is told in the Southwestern Yi treatise.

sentences.chinese: 自騫開外國道以尊貴，其吏士爭上書言外國奇怪利害，求使。
sentences.literal: From the time Qian opened the road to foreign states and became honored, his clerks and soldiers vied in submitting memorials speaking of foreign states' strangenesses, wonders, profits, and harms, seeking to be sent as envoys.
sentences.idiomatic: After Zhang Qian's opening of the western routes brought him rank and favor, officials and troops vied to memorialize about exotic lands, gains, and risks—all begging for an embassy.

sentences.chinese: 天子為其絕遠，非人所樂，聽其言，予節，募吏民無問所從來，為具備人眾遣之，以廣其道。
sentences.literal: The Son of Heaven because it was utterly distant, not what men delight in, listened to their words, gave them credentials, recruited clerks and commoners without asking whence they came, for them fully prepared men and masses and dispatched them, in order to broaden the road.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor knew the journey was grim and uninviting, yet he heard them out, issued staffs of office, and recruited clerks and commoners of any origin, fitting out full parties to widen the traffic in every direction.

sentences.chinese: 來還不能無侵盜幣物，及使失指，天子為其習之，輒復按致重罪，以激怒令贖，復求使。
sentences.literal: Coming back they could not but sometimes encroach on and steal goods and silks, and as for envoys missing the intent, the Son of Heaven because they were practiced in this, repeatedly again investigated and brought heavy crimes, in order to provoke anger and order ransom, again seeking to be envoys.
sentences.idiomatic: Returning missions could not help embezzling part of the imperial gifts, or bungling their instructions; the emperor, used to the pattern, would indict them on grave charges, let them buy their way out, and watch them volunteer for another posting.

sentences.chinese: 使端無窮，而輕犯法。
sentences.literal: The pretexts for missions were endless, while they lightly violated the law.
sentences.idiomatic: Excuses for new embassies multiplied, and men flouted the statutes with little fear.

sentences.chinese: 其吏卒亦輒復盛推外國所有，言大者予節，言小者為副，故妄言無行之徒皆爭相效。
sentences.literal: Their clerks and soldiers also repeatedly again greatly exaggerated what foreign states possessed; those whose words were large were given credentials, those whose words were small became deputies; therefore those who falsely spoke and were without conduct all vied to imitate one another.
sentences.idiomatic: Subordinates too puffed up the riches of the west: the grander the tale, the likelier the staff of office; smaller boasts won deputy posts—so liars and riffraff rushed to copy one another.
